question.text: A commodity producer who will sell output in the future should adopt:

answer.text: A short hedge — sell futures to lock in the selling price
A long hedge — buy futures contracts
Buy call options to protect against price decline
Wait until harvest to decide on hedging

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A producer needs to SELL output (naturally long). Short hedge (selling futures) locks in the selling price. If prices fall, futures profit offsets lower spot revenue. **The Trap:** Long hedge is for buyers, not sellers. Call options protect against rising prices, not falling. Waiting removes the protective benefit of hedging. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII covers short hedging for portfolio sellers. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Farmers should initiate short hedges during sowing season when futures prices may be favorable — waiting until harvest means hedging at already-depressed seasonal prices.
